evidence: Uses __slots__, validated blend-mode enum, zeroed float64 numpy storage, strength clamped to [0,1]. Functional but missing mask/visibility/dirty-rect channels that AAA layer systems carry. B+ is fair.
Serializes entire heightmap as Python list via tolist() -- no compression, no npz fallback, no schema version. For a 512x512 layer that is 256K JSON floats. C is accurate.
Mirrors to_dict, uses defaults for missing fields, reshapes. No schema versioning, no data length validation, would crash on missing 'data' key. C+ is fair.
Correct Catmull-Rom-style tangent estimation for cubic Bezier segments. Handles endpoint conditions properly. Clean math, configurable tension. A- is fair.
Textbook cubic Bezier evaluation with correct Bernstein polynomial expansion. Simple, correct, nothing to criticize.
Deduces grid dims from unique x/y coordinates with sqrt fallback. Rounding to 3 decimals is fragile for large terrains with close vertex spacing. Functional but hacky. B- fair.

dispute_reason: Pure-Python double loop (lines 566-598) over every cell in the brush rect is a performance catastrophe on any grid over ~128x128. The 'smooth' op uses a 3x3 box average (not Gaussian), 'noise' uses random.gauss per cell (spatially incoherent white noise, not simplex/perlin), and 'stamp' destructively replaces rather than blending. Gemini's A is absurd; Codex's B is too generous. Opus's original C+ was closest to reality.
evidence: Lines 566-598: nested Python for loops, 3x3 neighbor average for smooth, random.gauss() for noise (no spatial coherence), stamp just sets height = weight. AAA terrain brushes use vectorized numpy, Gaussian kernels, and spatially coherent noise.

dispute_reason: Line 1312: 'blend = edge_falloff * (1.0 - falloff) + edge_falloff * falloff' simplifies algebraically to just 'blend = edge_falloff' -- the falloff parameter is completely inert (dead parameter bug). Nearest-integer stamp sampling (no bilinear), additive only (no blend modes), no rotation support. Gemini's A is wrong. Consensus B- is generous given the dead-parameter bug.
evidence: Line 1312: edge_falloff*(1-f) + edge_falloff*f = edge_falloff*(1-f+f) = edge_falloff. The falloff param does nothing. Also nearest-neighbor sampling at lines 1306-1307. Pure Python loop (1293-1314). C is the correct grade.

dispute_reason: Pure-Python triple-nested loop (iterations * rows * cols * 4 neighbors) makes this unusable for grids > ~128. Uses only 4-neighborhood (no diagonals) so scree fans appear axis-aligned. Returns .tolist() (line 1184) bloating memory for 512+ maps. Codex/Gemini B+ is too generous -- this is a correct but slow and limited talus algorithm.
evidence: Lines 1153-1182: Python for r in range / for c in range / for dr,dc in offsets triple loop. 4-neighborhood only (offsets at line 1151). Returns hmap.tolist() at line 1184. No numpy vectorization, no diagonal neighbors, no per-material talus.

dispute_reason: Hydraulic erosion (lines 863-873) is a 4-neighbor slope-redistribution stencil with hardcoded 0.1 transfer factor -- no sediment transport, no water channels, no particle simulation, nothing resembling Mei-Hill or Hans-Beyer. Wind erosion (lines 888-894) is literally random.gauss() with fixed +X deposit direction. These are toy implementations, not erosion models. Gemini's A+ is flatly wrong; Codex's B ignores the fundamental inadequacy. Opus's C- was the honest grade.
evidence: Hydraulic: lines 863-873, simple diff*0.1 transfer to 4 neighbors. Wind: lines 888-894, gauss(0,0.3) removal + deposit one cell to the right. No sediment channels, no water depth, no velocity field, no directional wind vector. Pure Python triple loop (iterations * cells * neighbors).

dispute_reason: Only 4 hardcoded falloff curves with no user-definable curve editor or additional presets (gaussian, root, sphere, inverse-square). The clamp to 1.5 silently allows out-of-range input. Functional but basic -- Gemini's A overstates it, B is the right call.
evidence: 4 lambda functions in _FALLOFF_FUNCS dict (smooth/sharp/linear/constant). No LUT, no custom curve, no Bezier editor. Clamp at line 274 allows 1.5 max which is odd.

dispute_reason: Three nested Python loops (flow direction O(R*C*8), accumulation O(R*C), basin tracing O(R*C*path)) make this prohibitively slow for any grid above ~256. D8 has known artifacts (parallel stripes). No pit-filling pre-pass so internal pits create spurious basins. Returns .tolist() (line 1109) which bloats memory. The algorithm is textbook-correct but the implementation is impractical. Codex A- and Gemini A are grading the algorithm, not the implementation.
evidence: Lines 1026-1039: Python for r/c/d_idx triple loop for flow direction. Lines 1048-1059: Python loop over sorted flat indices. Lines 1066-1106: Python loop with while-trace per cell. All returns via .tolist() at line 1109. No numpy vectorization, no pit-fill, D8 only.

dispute_reason: Four modes (carve/raise/flatten/smooth) but 'smooth' mode (line 371-372) is just a weak pull-toward-spline-Z with weight*0.3 -- no actual Laplacian or neighborhood averaging. No cross-section profile (flat bottom only), no bank angles, no cutbanks. O(N*M) where N=vertices and M=polyline segments with no spatial acceleration. Gemini's A is inflated.
evidence: Smooth mode at line 371: 'new_z = vz + (closest[2] - vz) * weight * 0.3' -- just interpolates toward spline height with hardcoded 0.3 factor. No lateral profile, no true smoothing kernel. O(V*S) brute force at lines 339-376.

dispute_reason: Six named stamps (crater/mesa/hill/valley/plateau/ridge) are all trivially analytic radial functions -- single-line lambdas at lines 1193-1198. A real crater needs asymmetric rim, ejecta rays, central peak. A mesa needs fracture patterns. Fixed 64px resolution truncates detail. Custom passthrough is just np.asarray. Codex A- and Gemini A are grading the API surface, not the stamp quality. Opus C was closest.
evidence: Lines 1193-1198: 6 one-line lambda functions. crater = max(0, 1-abs(r*2-1)), mesa = 1 if r<0.6 else ramp, hill = cos(r*pi/2), etc. Fixed 64px at line 1356. No noise overlay, no asymmetry, no geologically motivated shapes.

dispute_reason: Correct XY-projected closest-point with cumulative t output and degenerate handling. But it is O(N) brute-force scan over every polyline segment per query -- AAA uses spatial acceleration (BVH/grid). XY-only projection is incorrect for vertical splines. Gemini's A ignores the performance issue that makes this impractical for dense meshes.
evidence: Lines 204-242: Linear scan over every segment. No BVH, no uniform grid, no spatial partitioning. Correct math but O(N) per query, O(V*N) for all vertices.

dispute_reason: Clean Catmull-Rom-style Bezier evaluation but uses fixed uniform parameter sampling -- no arc-length reparameterization, so point density clumps on tight curves and spreads on straights. AAA spline tools sample by arc length. No adaptive subdivision for curvature. Gemini A is too generous.
evidence: Lines 153-157: uniform t = i/samples_per_segment sampling with no arc-length correction. Functional but produces uneven point spacing which matters for road/river width consistency.

dispute_reason: Proper ordered blend with ADD/SUBTRACT/MAX/MIN/MULTIPLY modes. But nearest-neighbor resize (lines 624-635) produces blocky aliasing. MULTIPLY is idiosyncratic (1+lh, line 648). No OVERLAY/SCREEN/SOFT-LIGHT modes. No per-layer mask channel. Gemini A overstates it.
evidence: Nearest-neighbor resize at lines 624-635 using integer index mapping. MULTIPLY at line 648: result * (1.0 + lh) -- non-standard. Missing overlay/screen/soft-light blend modes. No mask support.

dispute_reason: Sequential application means later zones fight earlier ones in overlap regions (last-writer-wins). No overlap resolution, no priority system, no spatial bucketing. O(Z*H*W) with Z zones. Trivially simple wrapper. Codex A- and Gemini A are grading a 10-line for loop too generously.
evidence: Lines 1580-1590: Simple for loop calling flatten_terrain_zone sequentially. No overlap detection, no priority, no combined mask approach. 10 lines of code.

dispute_reason: Good vectorized numpy with smoothstep C1 blend, auto target height, and proper NaN/inf scrubbing. However, circular only (no ellipse/rectangle), isotropic blend, and seed parameter is unused dead code. Gemini A+ is excessive for a circular-only flatten. Consensus A- is slightly generous.
evidence: Lines 1529-1561: numpy vectorized, smoothstep blend (3t^2-2t^3), source range preservation. But circular only (line 1532: single radius), seed unused (line 1506 docstring says 'reserved'). Good but not A-tier.

dispute_reason: Thin wrapper around compute_erosion_brush which is itself C- quality. Inherits all the toy hydraulic/wind/thermal problems. No undo, blocks Blender main thread during slow Python erosion, doesn't rebuild normals after displacement. The handler adds nothing to fix the core weakness.
evidence: Lines 912-981: Standard bmesh read/write wrapper. Calls compute_erosion_brush (graded C-) without adding any value. No normals update, no undo push, no async.

dispute_reason: Proper world/local matrix handling and axis-angle normal alignment. But single downward ray with no fallback (misses overhangs), normal alignment replaces entire rotation_euler destroying authored yaw (line 1471), no multi-sample averaging, no parallelization. Gemini A is too generous.
evidence: Line 1471: obj.rotation_euler = rot_mat.to_euler() -- overwrites all three Euler angles including yaw. Single ray_cast at line 1448 with no fallback. No footprint averaging for noisy terrain.

dispute_reason: Wraps compute_spline_deformation with bmesh round-trip. But spline_points at line 413 are used as-is without matrix_world transform -- if the terrain is moved/scaled/rotated, the spline points are in the wrong space. No undo support. Gemini A- is inflated given the missing transform.
evidence: Line 413: spline_points = [tuple(p[:3]) for p in spline_points_raw] -- no matrix_world.inverted() transform. Lines 420-436: standard bmesh boilerplate. No undo_push.

dispute_reason: Full CRUD routing is good, but every mutation re-serializes the entire layer stack to JSON on a custom property (line 711, 719, 749). This bloats .blend files and will fail at ID-property size limits. flatten_layers assumes vertex order equals row-major reshape. No undo integration. B is too generous.
evidence: Lines 711, 719, 749: json.dumps([L.to_dict() for L in layers]) on every operation. flatten at line 761 uses reshape(rows, cols) assuming row-major vertex order. No undo.

dispute_reason: Standard bmesh wrapper reusing compute_stamp_heightmap + apply_stamp_to_heightmap. Inherits the dead-falloff-parameter bug from apply_stamp_to_heightmap, hardcoded 64x64 resolution (line 1356), no rotation, uses obj.dimensions which breaks when mesh is transformed. Gemini A- is wrong.
evidence: Line 1356: compute_stamp_heightmap(stamp_type, 64, ...) -- hardcoded 64. Inherits apply_stamp_to_heightmap's inert falloff bug. Lines 1366-1369: terrain_size=(dims.x, dims.y), terrain_origin=(obj.location.x, obj.location.y) -- no matrix_world.

dispute_reason: Handles arbitrary non-grid meshes by binning into a grid, which is good design. But grid binning loses sub-cell detail, delta lookup is nearest-neighbor (no bilinear), two dead lines at 1696-1700 (_ = max(..., 1e-6)), and radius_x/radius_y collapses to averaged flat_radius (line 1681) so ellipse support is fake. Gemini A is absurd for code with dead lines and fake ellipse params.
evidence: Line 1681: flat_radius = (norm_rx + norm_ry) * 0.5 -- collapses ellipse to circle. Lines 1696, 1700: _ = max(..., 1e-6) -- dead code. Nearest-neighbor at line 1703: grid[row_idx, col_idx].
Clean factory function: loads archetype defaults, applies user overrides, returns typed dataclass. Well-designed API. A is fair.
Standard coordinate conversion with clamping. Correct and simple. A- is fair.
Inverse of _world_to_cell with 0.5 cell-center offset. Correct.
Delegates to BBox.to_cell_slice -- clean one-liner. A is fair.
Vectorized numpy meshgrid with proper zone.permits('caves') check. Clean protected-zone implementation. B+ fair.
Multi-factor scoring (altitude, slope, wetness, basin, concavity) with deterministic jitter tiebreak. Heuristics are reasonable but hand-tuned magic numbers. Between B and C+, B- feels right.

dispute_reason: Per-archetype path shape is good design, but all paths are simple 2D polylines with trigonometric jitter -- no branching, no chamber nodes, no inclination variation within archetype. FISSURE just does uniform descent, KARST is vertical then horizontal, GLACIAL is sin() meander. Gemini A- overstates these simple parametric curves.
evidence: Lines 373-428: Each archetype is a simple for loop with linear/sinusoidal interpolation. No branching, no chamber nodes, no Y-splits, no 3D complexity beyond gentle z-descent.

dispute_reason: Vectorized np.mgrid footprint per waypoint is good. But this is a 2D heightmap-lowering operation -- it can only lower the terrain surface, not create true 3D cave interiors. You cannot represent a cave ceiling or passage with this approach. For AAA caves you need voxels or mesh booleans. The fundamental limitation is more severe than B+ suggests.
evidence: Lines 471-488: np.mgrid per waypoint, delta applied to 2D heightmap. Line 488: 'delta = np.where(footprint & (local_delta < delta), local_delta, delta)' -- only lowers surface. Cannot represent a cave roof or enclosed volume. Fundamental 2.5D limitation.

dispute_reason: Framing rocks are just world positions + radius -- no actual geometry, no shape variation, no per-archetype material hint, no mesh generation. It returns metadata (intent) but the actual visual result depends entirely on downstream consumers. Codex A- and Gemini A are grading the metadata design, not the visual output.
evidence: Lines 522-533: framing_rocks are dicts with 'world_pos' and 'radius_m'. No vertex data, no mesh, no shape. Purely metadata. Vegetation_screen is a boolean. Occlusion_shelf is dimension numbers.

dispute_reason: Deterministic, density-scaled, capped at 200 points. But no clustering (real debris piles up), no collision with entrance frame, all debris are the same type (just position tuples). Gemini A is too generous for what is essentially a random scatter along a path.
evidence: Lines 593-608: Random path segment + lateral jitter. No Poisson clustering, no size/type variation, no collision checks. Returns bare (x,y,z) tuples.
Vectorized np.mgrid per waypoint with max-merge. Preserves existing wet_rock. Linear falloff is simplistic (real seepage follows downslope) but functional. B+ is fair.

dispute_reason: Clean validation with hard/soft issue classification. But only 4 checks (framing count, lip height, damp mask, occlusion shelf). No geometry validation, no overlap detection, no terrain-context checks (is the entrance facing a cliff? is it underwater?). A is slightly generous for 4 simple threshold checks.
evidence: Lines 681-731: 4 checks -- framing count >= min, lip >= 1m, wet_rock populated, shelf depth > 0. No spatial validation, no context-aware checks.
Sources from scene_read.cave_candidates with region filter. Falls back to nothing if no scene_read (no auto-discovery from heightmap). B is fair for this simple filter function.

End-to-end pipeline: entrance candidates, per-cave deterministic seed, protected zone checks, archetype selection, path+carve+frame+debris+damp, cave_candidate masking, side_effects, validation, PassResult with metrics. Well-structured pass. Only processes scene_read entrances (no auto-discovery) limits it. B+ is fair.
Clean pass registration with proper channel declarations, seed_namespace, and metadata. Nothing to criticize.

dispute_reason: Uses cave_candidate channel to find positions, delegates to generate_cave_entrance_mesh. But sampling is random choice with no spatial distribution check -- could cluster entrances. Width/height/depth are random uniform with no archetype awareness. Between B and B+, B is fairer.
evidence: Lines 934-950: rng.choice for entrance positions, rng.uniform for width/height/depth. No minimum spacing, no archetype-driven sizing.
Constructs minimal viable TerrainPipelineState with flat heightmap + seeded noise, single cave candidate at center, proper scene_read wiring. Well-documented purpose. B+ fair.

dispute_reason: It creates a simple 8-vertex box mesh. Correctly documented as a marker/parent object, not visible geometry. But the mesh itself is trivial -- A- suggests production quality for what is fundamentally a bpy.data.meshes.new with 8 hardcoded verts. B+ is fairer for clean code that does a simple thing well.
evidence: Lines 1094-1124: 8 hardcoded verts, 6 faces for a box. Clean bpy boilerplate with try/except for test environments. Documented as invisible marker mesh.
MCP handler adapter bridging compose_map dispatch to pass_caves. Clean error handling, structured return payload. Uses synthetic state (not real scene), chamber mesh is a simple box. B is fair.
Standard FBM with opensimplex, configurable octaves/persistence/lacunarity, proper normalization. Correct implementation. A- fair.
Opensimplex noise wrapper with seed-based caching via global state. Functional but the global mutable state (_features_gen, _features_seed) is not thread-safe. B+ fair.

dispute_reason: Canyon is a straight rectilinear trench along X with only mild noise on walls. No meander, no talus slopes, no stratified rock layers. Side caves are metadata-only dicts (lines 213-229) with zero actual mesh carved. Material banding is just height-based (lower quarter = wet_rock). Resolution of floor is size/2 which is extremely coarse. Codex B is too generous.
evidence: Lines 122-144: straight floor along X axis. Lines 148-172: flat wall panels with noise displacement. Lines 213-229: side_caves are pure metadata dicts with no geometry. Resolution at line 107: res_along = max(4, int(length/2)) -- for a 50m canyon, only 25 segments.

dispute_reason: Ledges are literally single quads (8 verts for top+front, lines 362-377) with no side faces, no bottom. Pool is a crude 16-sided fan disc (lines 385-398) with no lip or undercut. Cave behind is pure metadata (lines 414-427) with no geometry carved. NO WATER MESH at all -- the waterfall has no actual water. Direction-aware rotation is a nice feature but cannot redeem the fundamental lack of water geometry. Codex A- is egregiously wrong.
evidence: Lines 362-377: ledge = 8 verts, 2 faces (top quad + front quad). Lines 385-398: pool = 16-gon fan disc. Lines 414-427: cave = metadata dict. No water mesh anywhere in the function. The function called 'generate_waterfall' generates no water.

dispute_reason: Two-octave noise on face is good. Overhang lean at top quartile with underside sheet is structurally sound. But cave entrances are metadata-only (lines 619-632, no holes punched), and the ledge is a flat two-vertex strip with no thickness or 3D walkable geometry (lines 648-658). The overhang underside is only 4 strips deep. Codex B is too generous for metadata caves and flat ribbon ledges.
evidence: Lines 619-632: cave_entrances are dicts with position/width/height/depth -- no geometry. Lines 648-658: ledge = 2 vertices per waypoint, single quad strip. Overhang underside at lines 593-616: only 4 depth samples. No boolean cave cuts.

dispute_reason: Resolution is size/2 (line 726), so a 50m swamp is only 25x25 = 625 quads at ~2m spacing -- far too coarse for AAA. No drainage channels, no peat morphology. Water is tagged via material only -- no actual water plane mesh. BFS flood fill for water zones is good but the underlying terrain is too low-res. Codex B+ ignores the crippling resolution issue.
evidence: Line 726: resolution = max(8, int(size / 2)) -- 50m swamp = 25x25 grid. Lines 762-769: hummock application via O(N^2) double loop per hummock. No water surface mesh, water indicated only by material index.

dispute_reason: The arch is a swept elliptical tube (torus-arc) with per-ring noise -- the textbook 'stretched torus' approach. Real natural arches are eroded rock masses with flat tops, scalloped undersides, and asymmetric pillar bases. Pillars are tapered 4-vert boxes (lines 1054-1067). This is a bent pipe, not an eroded sandstone arch. Codex B is too generous. Between Opus D+ and consensus C+, I place it at C-.
evidence: Lines 970-1016: semi-elliptical sweep with ring cross-section. Each ring is an ellipse with noise. Pillars at lines 1054-1067: 4-corner cross-section with slight taper. No flat top surface, no scalloped underside, no asymmetric erosion.

dispute_reason: Pool bottom is a flat fan disc (not concave despite comment), terraces are 3 rigid concentric rings of equal width (not scalloped basins), vent cone is just 3 rings (base/mid/tip). No water surface mesh, no steam vent positions, no pour-lips. Better than C because the 3-tier structure shows intent, but not B- quality.
evidence: Lines 1159-1177: pool is fan triangles from center to edge ring -- flat disc. Lines 1232-1268: 3 concentric rings with fixed mineral_rim_width spacing. Lines 1181-1223: vent = base ring + mid ring + tip vertex.

dispute_reason: Multi-ring wall construction with FBM roughness and material banding is good. Rubble pieces are real 8-vert displaced boxes (lines 1460-1483). But walls are rotationally symmetric (only 15% narrowing at bottom, line 1396), bottom cave is metadata-only (lines 1487-1503), no water/vegetation shelf despite sinkholes typically having both. Codex B+ is too generous.
evidence: Line 1396: r_at_depth = radius * (1.0 - kt * 0.15) -- only 15% narrowing, axisymmetric. Lines 1487-1503: cave_info is a dict with no geometry. Lines 1460-1483: rubble boxes are real mesh but axis-aligned.

dispute_reason: Latitude/longitude tessellated spheres with noise are fundamentally noisy spheres, not fractured rock polyhedra. Chain links are open cylindrical shafts (4-vert rings, lines 1710-1726), not interlocking torus rings. No flat-top island surface, no crystal geometry (crystal_vein material only). Consensus B- is slightly generous for what are noisy spheres with cylindrical tubes.
evidence: Lines 1601-1633: latitude-ring sphere with noise displacement. Lines 1710-1726: chain link = 2 rings of 4 verts connected by 4 quads -- an open cylinder, not a chain link shape.

dispute_reason: Material banding in stalactites uses 'kt' variable (line 1867-1872) which references the OUTER loop variable 'k' from the cone_rings loop, but inside the face-generation loop (line 1858) kt is NOT updated -- it retains the last value from the vertex generation loop. This is a variable scoping bug. Additionally: refraction_zones (lines 1925-1930) are randomly sampled positions disconnected from actual curvature classification. Ice wall is flat (no thickness). No stalagmites, no pool, no volumetric ice. Codex B ignores the kt bug.
evidence: Line 1867: 'if kt < 0.3' -- but kt was set at line 1837 (k / max(cone_rings-1, 1)) in the VERTEX loop, and is NOT recalculated in the FACE loop starting at line 1858. kt retains its last value from the vertex loop (always ~1.0 for the last ring). Material banding is therefore broken.

dispute_reason: Sinusoidal centerline with tangent-perpendicular cross-section is well-structured. Five-band material zones (hot_lava/ember_glow/cooling_crust/solid_rock) with height profile is good design. But it is a single non-branching ribbon strip, crust edges are symmetric, lava surface is the same mesh as rock banks (no separate flowing surface), and heat zones are uniformly distributed with no flow-physics grounding. Codex B+ overstates it.
evidence: Lines 2025-2079: single strip sweep along sinusoidal path. No branching. Lines 2059-2070: symmetric height profile. Lines 2106-2121: heat_zones uniformly spaced along t. No separate lava surface mesh.

dispute_reason: Same 4 hardcoded curves as terrain_advanced.compute_falloff (smooth/sharp/linear/constant). No user-definable curve, no gaussian/root/sphere presets, clamp to 1.5 allows out-of-range values silently. Gemini A- is inflated. Between C+ and B, B- is fair -- it works but is basic.
evidence: Lines 30-35: 4 lambda functions identical to terrain_advanced._FALLOFF_FUNCS. Line 53: clamp to 1.5 max. No curve editor, no additional presets.

dispute_reason: O(N) linear scan over EVERY vertex with no spatial acceleration (KD-tree/grid). XY-only distance projection is wrong on sloped terrain. No pressure/tilt/azimuth, no elliptical brush, no alpha mask, no stroke dab spacing. For a mesh with 100K+ vertices, this scans all of them for every dab. Codex A- is absurd for an O(N) brute-force scan with no spatial index. Opus C- was closest.
evidence: Lines 83-93: for i, (vx, vy) in enumerate(vert_positions_2d) -- iterates every vertex. No KD-tree, no grid hash, no BVH. XY-only distance at line 87-88.

dispute_reason: Displaces purely along world Z. No surface-normal displacement option, no accumulation curve, no max-height clamp, no layer/undo stack, no pressure sensitivity, no noise/jitter. AAA raise brushes offset along averaged vertex normal with stroke accumulation and clamp. This is height[i] += strength * weight, nothing more. Codex A and Gemini A are grading a 3-line function as professional-grade, which is wrong. It is correct but trivially basic.
evidence: Lines 112-115: result[idx] = vert_heights[idx] + strength * w. That is the entire computation. 3 lines of math. No normal direction, no clamp, no accumulation.

dispute_reason: Identical to raise but with subtraction. Same gaps: no normal alignment, no floor clamp, no distinct dig behavior. Codex A for a sign-flip of raise is absurd grading.
evidence: Lines 123-124: result[idx] = vert_heights[idx] - strength * w. Literally compute_raise with a minus sign.

dispute_reason: Uses real 1-ring adjacency which is good (not a fixed-radius hack). But only smooths Z (not full 3D), uniform weighting (no cotangent/area weights, causes shrinkage), single Jacobi iteration per call (no iteration count param), no Taubin lambda/mu shrinkage compensation, no boundary handling. Blender/ZBrush offer Laplacian, Surface, Enhance-Detail, Taubin, and relax modes. Codex B+ and Gemini A are wildly generous.
evidence: Lines 149-157: avg_z = sum(neighbors_z) / len(neighbors). Single iteration, uniform weights, Z-only. No iteration count, no cotangent weights, no Taubin, no detail-preserving mode.

dispute_reason: Flattens to unweighted mean Z of affected verts (not a fitted plane). Ignores brush weights in the average (line 172: plain indices, no weight). No user-set target height, no eye-dropper pick, no plane-from-first-contact trim mode, no angle/slope constraint. AAA flatten brushes do least-squares plane fit. Codex A- is extremely generous for an unweighted mean.
evidence: Lines 171-172: indices = [idx for idx, _ in weights]; avg_height = sum(heights[idx]) / len(indices). Weights are used for blending (line 177) but NOT for computing the target height. No plane fitting.

dispute_reason: Nearest-neighbor sampling only (lines 226-227: int rounding), causing blocky artifacts. Axis-aligned square footprint inside a circular brush. No rotation, no scale, no flip, no blend modes (add/max/min/subtract/replace), no height remap curve. AAA stamps support rotation, 8+ blend ops, bicubic sampling. Codex B and Gemini B are too generous.
evidence: Lines 226-227: col = min(int(u * (cols-1) + 0.5), cols-1); row = min(int(v * (rows-1) + 0.5), rows-1). No bilinear/bicubic interpolation. No rotation parameter. Only additive blending (line 230: heights[idx] + h_val * strength * w).
Standard bmesh adjacency construction via link_edges. Clean and correct. A- fair.

dispute_reason: Not a true stroke system -- single dab per call with full mesh read/write every time O(N). No undo stack, no symmetry, no multires integration, no mask/vertex-group, no normal updates. Clean bmesh lifecycle (try/finally) and validation are good but cannot mask the fundamental limitation. Gemini A- is very wrong.
evidence: Lines 282-338: Full bmesh read, extract all positions, compute one dab of weights, apply, write back, free. O(N) per call. No stroke accumulation, no undo_push, no symmetry planes, no dirty-vert optimization.
Simple incrementing ID allocator. Correct and clean.
Simple incrementing ID allocator. Correct and clean.
Simple incrementing ID allocator. Correct and clean.
Sqrt scaling for depth analogous to width. Correct hydraulic geometry relation. Min/max clamp. A- fair.
Simple coordinate transform. Correct.
Correct sqrt scaling (width ~ sqrt(discharge)) matching Leopold-Maddock hydraulic geometry relation. Min/max clamp. Scale factor as parameter. A- fair.

dispute_reason: Naive pit detection (cell < all 8 neighbors) misses plateau lakes where multiple cells share minimum height. Spill_z is set to min_neighbor_h which is the lowest single neighbor, not a true watershed spill elevation computed via priority-flood (Barnes 2014). The flood fill is correct but starts from a simplistic pit definition. B is more accurate than B+.
evidence: Lines 204-215: is_pit = True only if ALL 8 neighbors are strictly higher. Lines 222: spill_z = min_neighbor_h (lowest single neighbor, not watershed spill). No priority-flood algorithm.

dispute_reason: Sliding-window scan along a river path finding best drop is a reasonable heuristic. But only scans the given river_path (won't find falls off-path), best_drop is absolute drop not drop-rate (drop/horizontal_dist), and it only processes one path at a time. Codex A- is too generous for a simple sliding window.
evidence: Lines 298-331: sliding window with max_cells_ahead. best_drop = z_top - z_bot (absolute, not rate). Skips past detected falls (line 329: i = best_j + 1). Only processes explicit river_path input.
D8 descent with visited-set cycle guard, bounds checking, pit detection, accumulation-drop guard. Clean and correct algorithm. Python list for path is fine for single rivers. A- fair.
Finds cells above threshold with no upstream neighbor above threshold. Pure Python O(R*C*8) scan. Correct logic for identifying headwater sources. B fair.

Intersects river paths with tile grid lines to produce edge contracts. Uses midpoint interpolation (line 708: cross_r = (r0+r1)/2) which is approximate. Handles all 4 edge directions. Functional but the midpoint approximation and lack of precise intersection computation limits accuracy. C+ fair.
Correct Strahler ordering: DFS with memoization + cycle guard, proper merge rule (same-order tributaries -> +1). Good documentation. B+ fair.

dispute_reason: Uses setattr on a dataclass (line 1013) to inject strahler_order as a dynamic attribute, which is fragile and won't serialize via asdict(). The bare except pass at line 1014-1015 is a code smell. But the function is documented about these limitations and is idempotent. C is slightly harsh -- C+ is fairer given the documentation and the pragmatic approach.
evidence: Lines 1007-1016: Calls compute_strahler_orders then setattr(seg, 'strahler_order', ...) with try/except/pass. Won't appear in asdict() serialization. Documented limitations.
Simple dict lookup with default empty edges. Clean API. A fair.

Iterates all segments checking bounding box intersection. Correct run-based segment splitting. Could use spatial indexing for large networks but functional. Lines 881-882: dead variables (_ = self.nodes.get(...)). B fair.
Simple filter over compute_strahler_orders. Clean, well-documented. Recomputes orders each call (could cache) but correct. A- fair.
Serializes nodes, segments, and tile_contracts via dataclass asdict(). Includes version field. Clean but no compression, no external blob support. B fair.
Correct deserialization with tuple conversion for waypoints and flow_direction. Handles all fields with defaults. A- fair.
